title: Part I: Gervaise
paragraphs: Gervaise had waited for Lantier until two in the morning. Then, shivering, her limbs stiff from sitting in the cold on a chair, she finally fell asleep with her head against the wall.

She was twenty-two years old. She was small and slight, already a little lame from a fall she had suffered as a child in Plassans, which had made one hip higher than the other. But she was pretty enough, with her pale face and abundant ash-blond hair, and her eyes the color of clear water.

She had come to Paris three years before with Lantier—Auguste Lantier, a dyer's son from Plassans, who had promised her everything and given her two children and then mostly given her worry. He was handsome, Lantier, and knew it. He liked clothes and cafés and other women, and he liked Gervaise least when she was in most need of him.

She looked out the window of their cheap hotel room at the street below. The Rue Neuve de la Goutte-d'Or, Montmartre: working-class Paris, the Paris of the zinc-roofed tenements and the neighborhood laundries and the neighborhood grog-shop—the assommoir, the great drinking-shop on the corner where men stopped in before work and stayed through lunch and came home afterward smelling of absinthe.

She was not a complaining woman. She had learned early that complaining was useless and had developed a patient, practical endurance. She asked for little: work enough to eat, a clean place to sleep, and people who did not hurt her. She had come to Paris expecting to find these things, and had found instead the hotel room on the Rue Neuve de la Goutte-d'Or, and Lantier's debts, and the assommoir on the corner.
Lantier came back at noon. He had been out all night. He smelled of wine and another woman's perfume.
'Where were you?' she said.
'Out,' he said.
She looked at him. She did not argue. There was no point in arguing with Lantier.
'The children need shoes,' she said.
'I know,' he said. He sat down and put his head in his hands. 'I'll get some money.'

He did not get any money. Instead, a week later, he left her. He packed a bag one morning while she was at work at the laundry around the corner, and when she came home at noon the bag was gone and Lantier was gone and she was alone with two small boys in the hotel room on the Rue Neuve de la Goutte-d'Or.
She sat on the edge of the bed for a long time. Then she wiped her eyes and went to fetch the children from the neighbor's.

title: Part II: The Laundry
paragraphs: Gervaise had a friend named Virginie—a tall, dark-skinned girl who had also known Lantier, which made things complicated between them, but who was not entirely unkind. And she had a suitor named Coupeau—a roofer, a decent young man with a cheerful face, who came and went at the laundry and looked at her as though she were the best thing he had seen in some time.
She resisted Coupeau for a long while. She had two children and a distrust of men, reasonable after Lantier, and she had a dream.
'I want my own laundry,' she told Coupeau. 'A little place, just one room if necessary, but my own. Where I work for myself.'
'Marry me,' said Coupeau, 'and we'll have your laundry.'
She married him. He was a good husband, in the beginning—working, bringing home his pay, playing with the children. He did not drink, which was remarkable in the Rue Neuve de la Goutte-d'Or.

They saved money. They found a small shop on the Rue de la Goutte-d'Or itself, with a back room for pressing and a front room with big windows. Gervaise scrubbed it clean and set up her ironing table and hung her sign: Gervaise Coupeau, Laundress.

She was happy. She had never been happy before, not like this—not a calm, steady happiness that rose in the mornings with the steam from the tubs and lasted through the day. She had good customers. She worked hard. The laundry smelled of hot linen and violet water. In the evenings she put the children to bed and Coupeau would come home and they would sit by the lamp, and it was enough.
Then Coupeau fell off a roof.

It was an accident—the kind that happened often in his trade. He slipped and fell and broke his leg and cracked his ribs, and lay in bed for five months. Gervaise nursed him and kept the laundry running and paid the doctor and did not complain.

But Coupeau, when he got up, was different. He had been lying still for five months, and something had gone out of him—the steadiness, the purposefulness. He started going to the assommoir on the corner, first once a week, then every evening, then at noon as well.
'Just a little,' he told Gervaise. 'I need it for my nerves. Since the accident, my nerves—'

She watched it happen and did not know how to stop it. She was too busy with the laundry, with the children, with keeping everything going. And she kept thinking: he will stop. He has always been good. He will stop.
He did not stop.

title: Part III: The Decline
paragraphs: It happened slowly, and then all at once.

First the laundry began to suffer. Gervaise worked long hours but she was tired all the time—tired from worry, tired from carrying everything alone. She began to take in less work, because she could not keep up with it. She began to give credit to customers who did not pay her back.

Coupeau brought home less and less money. What he brought home he drank. He would come back from the assommoir late at night, his eyes glazed, and Gervaise would be waiting and he would look at her as though she were a stranger.

She started drinking too. She told herself it was just to keep warm, just to sleep, just to get through the day. The neighbor Mes-Bottes at the assommoir on the corner would offer her a glass—just a little, just to warm the stomach—and she would drink it and feel the warmth spread through her and for a little while not feel the laundry and the bills and Coupeau's vacant eyes.
Lantier came back.

He appeared one afternoon at the laundry as though he had never left, as though three years had not passed. He was handsome still. He smiled at Gervaise and she found, to her horror, that she could not be angry—that she was only tired and glad to see a face from before.

He stayed. He simply stayed—moved into a room in the building, ate at their table, borrowed money that was never returned. Coupeau, who should have thrown him out, seemed instead relieved to have someone to drink with. And Gervaise—Gervaise told herself it was for the children, because Lantier knew the children, because things were complicated.
The laundry closed. They had spent the savings, then sold the equipment. Gervaise took in washing by the piece. Then she could not even do that—her hands shook too much from the alcohol.
She went to work for other laundresses, women who once had been her customers. She scrubbed floors. She begged, finally, because there was nothing else.

The cold killed Coupeau—he died in a hospital, in the delirium tremens, seeing things on the walls. A month later Gervaise died too, in the stairwell of the tenement, where she had been sleeping when there was no room for her anywhere else.
No one on the Rue de la Goutte-d'Or found it particularly remarkable. The neighborhood was full of such stories. The assommoir on the corner was still there, its door always open.
The city went on. The zinc-roofed tenements went on. The steam from the laundries went on.
It was Paris, in the nineteenth century, for the people who had to live there.
